items.question: In addition to smoking cessation, initiation of which of the following would lead to the greatest reduction in his lifetime cardiovascular risk?
items.explanation:
## 5 ANSWER: D) Karyotype analysis

In women with primary ovarian insufficiency (previously termed premature ovarian failure), it is important to determine the underlying cause. The most common causes, after excluding radiation exposure, chemotherapy or ovarian surgery, are autoimmune etiologies and abnormal karyotypes, with Turner syndrome being the most common cause of primary ovarian insufficiency. In addition, expansion of the CGG repeats in the 5' untranslated region of the FMR1 gene is common in this patient population. When the number of CGG repeats expands to greater than 200, the gene is methylated and silenced and Fragile X syndrome can result. Women who carry 5' 200 repeats are at risk for primary ovarian insufficiency. Therefore, all women with primary ovarian insufficiency should have assessment of FMR1 repeat length, measurement of adrenal antibodies (which are indicative of autoimmunity against steroid-secreting cells in the adrenal and ovary), and karyotype analysis (Answer D). The patient's karvotype was 45.X, which is diagnostic of Turner syndrome.

Patient's karyotype was 45,X, which is diagnosed as a tumor of the X chromosome.

Turner syndrome classically results from loss of an X chromosome, but it can be caused by loss of the shown of the X chromosome or by mosaic 45,X/46,XX; 45,X,i(Xq) consisting of 2 long arms of the X chromosome, 46,XY; or 46,X,del(Xp), among others. In women with Turner syndrome and a 45,X karyotype, the�otype can be quite variable as demonstrated by this patient. In the classic presentation, affected patients have a long arm of the X chromosome, but it can be caused by loss of the X chromosome or by mosaic 45,X/46,XX; 45,X,i(Xq) consisting of 2 long arms of the X chromosome, 46,XY; or 46,X,del(Xp), among others. In women with Turner syndrome and a 45,X karyotype, the

fetuses with Turner syndrome, the complement of primordial germ cells is normal up to 12 weeks' gesta

subsequently decreased. Therefore, it appears that follicle atresia occurs more rapidly than normal and can

complete loss of oocytes before the onset of puberty. Ovaries may become fibrotic and appear as streak

sonography (Answer B) would not be helpful in this patient who had normal menstrual function and m

w has small ovaries on the basis of her high FSH level.

nts with Turner syndrome should be followed up for common medical complications. A baseline program is required for diagnosis of congenital left-sided cardiac defects and follow-up every 5 years with program or MRI is needed to evaluate for aortic dilatation. Other assessments should include hearing loss for renal anomalies, along with close follow-up for obesity, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, type 2 mellitus, celiac disease, osteoporosis, and liver function abnormalities. Hypothyroidism is also common syndrome, as it is in women with other causes of primary ovarian insufficiency. However, adrenal not thyroid antibodies (Answer A), are indicative of concomitant ovarian autoimmunity, and assess the need for the treatment of the patients who has already presented with hypothyroidism.

A gonadotropin-secreting pituitary adenoma in a woman of reproductive age may secrete functional FSH. The presentation would consist of ovarian enlargement from multiple follicle development (as seen with FSH stimulation during ovarian hyperstimulation for fertility treatment), elevated estradiol from multiple follicle development, and, possibly, slightly elevated prolactin due to pituitary stalk compression. This patient has high FSH, low estradiol, and normal prolactin; therefore, a pituitary-directed MRI (Answer C) is not warranted.

Short stature in women with Turner syndrome is related to loss of the SHOX gene on the short arm of the X chromosome. There is no need to workup a central cause of short stature with an IGF-1 measurement (Answer E) in a patient with an elevated FSH level and low estradiol level because the cause of amenorrhea is primary to the ovary.

##### Educ

items.question:
A 23-year-old woman comes to see you for evaluation of a 7-month history of amenorrhea. She stopped hormonal contraception 7 months ago because she thought taking hormones for many years was a bad idea. Her history is notable for irregular menses, every 3 months, starting at menarche at age 12 years. She states that she has never had an eating disorder. In high school, she participated in 3 sports, including track and cross-country. She continued running in college and began training for a half-marathon 8 months ago. She currently runs 30 to 40 miles per week. Acne appeared at age 13 years. Hormonal contraception was prescribed at age 14 years, which alleviated the acne and caused regular withdrawal bleeding, but her acne has returned since she stopped the medication. She has no symptoms of hirsutism, alopecia, galactorrhea, hypothyroidism, or hyperthyroidism. She is not sexually active.

On physical examination, blood pressure is 100/58 mm Hg. Height is 64 in (162.6 cm), and weight is 122 lb (54.5 kg) (BMI = 20.9 kg/m²). Acne lesions are present on her face and back. Her Ferriman-Gallwey score is 2, and there is no acanthosis nigricans or violaceous striae. Her thyroid gland is approximately 20 g and has no nodules. There is no galactorrhea. Pelvic examination reveals a normal uterus and ovaries.

Laboratory test results:

TSH = 1.01 mIU/L

Prolactin = 5.3 ng/mL (0.23 nmol/L)

Estradiol = 43 pg/mL (157.9 pmol/L)

Testosterone = 76 ng/dL (2.6 nmol/L)

FSH = 5.4 mIU/mL (5.4 IU/L)

DHEA-S = 150 µg/dL (4.1 µmol/L)

LH = 12.8 mIU/mL (12.8 IU/L)

17-Hydroxyprogesterone (8 AM) = 57 ng/dL (1.7 nmol/L)

Which of the following is the most likely cause of the amenorrhea and acne?
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Question 27: "## 27 ANSWER: A) Polycystic ovary syndrome"

items.title: Why answer A is correct
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Answer 27: "## 27 ANSWER: A) Polycystic ovary syndrome"

items.question:
A 27-year-old woman with a diagnosis of polycystic ovary syndrome comes to see you for fertility treatment. At her initial presentation several years ago, she had a history of menses every 3 months since menarche, hirsutism, and acne. She had been treated with hormonal contraception and spironolactone, with good results. These medications were stopped 6 months ago, and she had only 1 menstrual cycle. In addition to ordering a pregnancy test, which is negative, you prescribe a prenatal vitamin.

On physical examination, blood pressure is 118/62 mm Hg. Height is 61 in (154.9 cm), and weight is 122 lb (55.5 kg) (BMI = 23 kg/m $ ^{2} $). She has hair loss in the temporal area, a Ferriman-Gallwey score of 10, and no acanthosis nigricans. Her thyroid gland is approximately 20 g, and there are no nodules. Findings on breast and pelvic examinations are normal.

Laboratory test results:

FSH = 7.2 mIU/mL (7.2 IU/L)

Estradiol = 33 pg/mL (121.1 pmol/L)

Testosterone = 67 ng/dL (2.32 nmol/L)

TSH = 2.18 mIU/L

Oral glucose tolerance test:

Fasting glucose = 99 mg/dL (5.49 mmol/L)

Glucose 2 hours after 75-g glucose load = 101 mg/dL (5.61 mmol/L)

Fasting insulin = 13.7 μIU/mL (95.1 pmol/L)

Which of the following is the best first choice for fertility treatment in this patient?
items.explanation:
## 44 ANSWER: C) Clomiphene citrate Women with polycystic ovary syndrome

Women with polycystic ovary syndrome have infertility related to irregular menstrual cycles and anovulation. Clomiphene citrate (Answer C) is the best first-line medication to induce ovulation in this setting. Clomiphene citrate is a selective estrogen receptor modulator that inhibits estradiol's negative feedback at the hypothalamus, resulting in increased GnRH pulse frequency. The increased GnRH pulsatility stimulates LH and FSH, which then drive follicle growth and estradiol production. The improvement in pregnancy and live birth rate is significant when compared with outcomes using placebo.

Metformin (Answer B) is used off-label for polycystic ovary syndrome because it increases menstrual cycle frequency and pregnancy rate. However, the pregnancy rate is lower than with clomiphene citrate, and metformin does not improve the live birth rate. Therefore, it is not the treatment of choice for infertility. Further, the pregnancy rate using a combination of clomiphene citrate and metformin (Answer D) is not superior to clomiphene alone. In women who do not respond to treatment with clomiphene citrate, recombinant human FSH (Answer A) is the second-line treatment choice.

A recent meta-analysis demonstrated that letrozole, an aromatase inhibitor (Answer E), improves the pregnancy and live birth rate in women with polycystic ovary syndrome when compared with rates observed with clomiphene citrate. However, the quality of the evidence was deemed to be low and it was suggested that these results be regarded with caution. Therefore, an aromatase inhibitor is not yet the recommended treatment for ovulation induction in women with polycystic ovary syndrome until stronger evidence is available.

##### Educational Objective:

Select the most appropriate treatment for a woman with polycystic ovary syndrome who wants to become pregnant.

UpToDate Topic Review(s):

Treatment of polycystic ovary syndrome in adults

##### Reference(s):

Legro RS, Arslanian SA, Ehrmann DA, et al; Endocrine Society. Diagnosis and treatment of polycystic ovary syndrome: an Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2013;98(12):4565-4592.

Tang T, Lord JM, Norman RJ, Yasmin E, Balen AH. Insulin-sensitising drugs (metformin, rosiglitazone, pioglitazone, D-chiro-inositol) for women with polycystic ovary syndrome, oligo amenorrhoea and subfertility. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2012;5:CD003053.

Franik S, Kremer JA, Nelen WL, Farquhar C. Aromatase inhibitors for subfertile women with polycystic ovary syndrome. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2014;2:CD010287.

items.question:
A 20-year-old woman with polycystic ovary syndrome seeks advice regarding treatment of acne, hirsutism, and irregular menses. Her menstrual cycles have been irregular (3 to 4 cycles per year) since menarche at age 12 years. Acne has been a problem since puberty. She also notes hair growth on her upper lip, chin, abdomen, and upper thighs. She has a history of migraine headaches with a visual aura, which begins with a flashing light or a black area in the visual fields that spreads. She has not been treated for polycystic ovary syndrome in the past, but her acne and hirsutism have worsened and she would like definitive treatment. She is sexually active, but does not desire pregnancy.

On physical examination, blood pressure is 110/60 mm Hg. Weight is 114 lb (51.8 kg), and height is 62.5 in (158.8 cm) (BMI = 20.5 kg/m $ ^{2} $). On skin examination, she has cystic acne on her chin, a Ferriman-Gallwey score of 12, and no acanthosis nigricans.

Laboratory test results:

Testosterone = 63 ng/dL (2.19 nmol/L)

DHEA-S = 257  $ \mu $g/dL (6.9  $ \mu $mol/L)

17-Hydroxyprogesterone (8 AM) = 40 ng/dL (1.21 nmol/L)

Which of the following treatments should you recommend for this woman with polycystic ovary syndrome?
options: Levonorgestrel-coated intrauterine device D.Combination hormonal contraception
items.explanation:
## 60 ANSWER: E) Spironolactone and norethindrone-only hormonal contraception

60 The risk of stroke is increased in patients with migraines and visual aura compared with the risk in patients who have migraines without aura. A concerning visual aura includes a zigzag line that moves laterally in the visual field or an area of lost vision that spreads laterally surrounded by normal visual fields, as described in the vignette. The etiology of a visual aura may be ischemia, which could underlie the increased risk for stroke. Studies suggest that the risk of stroke is 2 to 4 times higher for women with migraines taking oral contraceptives than those without, but no studies have had sufficient numbers to examine women with aura vs those without aura. The risk of stroke is so low in women of reproductive age that migraines without aura are not a contraindication to hormonal contraception as long as a woman is healthy, younger than 35 years, and does not smoke. In contrast, migraines with aura at any age are a contraindication for combined estrogen and progestin hormonal contraception (Answer D).

In this vignette, the patient wants treatment for hirsutism and acne. While combined estrogen and progestin is the treatment of choice for acne and hirsutism, the therapy is contraindicated in this patient. Spironolactone and norethindrone-only contraception (Answer E) would be the best combination to treat her primary symptoms.

Spironolactone, used off-label for polycystic ovary syndrome, alleviates acne and hirsutism through blockade of androgen receptor in the skin. Progestin-only contraception, which does not exacerbate migraines, protects the uterus from unopposed endogenous estrogen exposure. It also prevents pregnancy, which is important when spironolactone is used because it causes ambiguous genitalia in male fetuses in animal models and is classified as a pregnancy category C drug.

Although progestin-only contraception in the form of a subcutaneous depot (medroxyprogesterone), pill (norethindrone), or coated intrauterine device (levonorgestrel) (Answer C) would all be acceptable alternatives for contraception and uterine protection, they do not address her primary concern when used alone. Similarly, monthly micronized progesterone (Answer A) protects the uterus from unopposed estrogen exposure, but does not address the cosmetic concerns or the possibility of pregnancy. Metformin (Answer B) can increase the number of ovulatory menstrual cycles in women with polycystic ovary syndrome, but it is not a good treatment for hirsutism or acne.

##### Educational Objective:

Select the most appropriate treatment for cosmetic symptoms and irregular menses in a woman with polycystic ovary syndrome and complicated migraines.

##### UpToDate Topic Review(s):

Treatment of polycystic ovary syndrome in adults

##### Reference(s):

Petitti DB. Combination estrogen-progestin oral contraceptives [published correction appears in N Engl J Med. 2004;350(1):92]. N Engl J Med. 2003;349(15):1443-1450.

ACOG Committee on Practice Bulletins-Gynecology. ACOG practice bulletin. No. 73: Use of hormonal contraception in women with coexisting medical conditions. Obstet Gynecol. 2006;107(6):1453-1472.

Division of Reproductive Health, National Center for Chronic Disease Prevention and Health Promotion, Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC).

U.S. Selected Practice Recommendations for Contraceptive Use, 2013: adapted from the World Health Organization selected practice recommendations for contraceptive use, 2nd edition. MMWR Recomm Rep. 2013;62(RR-05):1-60.

items.question:
A 15-year-old girl presents with primary amenorrhea. She underwent adrenarche at age 9 years with axillary and pubic hair development and thelarche at age 10 years. However, she has not undergone menarche, with the exception of scant vaginal bleeding on one occasion at age 13 years. She has developed acne and hirsutism along with cramping and mood changes suggestive of hormone production. She has also been overweight since age 9 years, and her BMI is at the 92nd percentile.

On physical examination, blood pressure is 110/80 mm Hg. Weight is 220 lb (100 kg), and height is 68.5 in (174 cm) (BMI = 33 kg/m²). On skin examination, she has mild acne, as well as acanthosis nigricans on the neck and in the axillae. Her breasts and pubic hair are Tanner stage 5.

Laboratory test results:

TSH = 3.72 mIU/L

Prolactin = 8.6 ng/mL (373.9 pmol/L)

Sex hormone-binding globulin = 1.91  $ \mu $g/mL (17.0 nmol/L)

FSH = 6.7 mIU/mL (6.7 IU/L)

DHEA-S = 285 μg/dL (7.7 μmol/L)

LH = 14.3 mIU/mL (14.3 IU/L)

17-Hydroxyprogesterone 126 ng/dL (3.82 nmol/L)

Testosterone = 63 ng/dL (2.19 nmol/L)

β-hCG = <6 mIU/mL (<6 IU/L)

Pelvic ultrasonography reveals ovarian volumes of 17 mL (right ovary) and 14 mL (left ovary) and a normal uterus with an endometrial thickness of 5.8 mm.

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Question 72: "See ESAP 2015 discussion."

items.title: Why answer A is correct
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Answer 72: "See ESAP 2015 discussion."

items.question:
You are caring for a 54-year-old woman on hormone therapy for menopausal symptoms. During perimenopause, the patient noticed hot flashes, difficulty sleeping, hair loss, depression, abdominal weight gain, and poor concentration, with worsening of symptoms after she developed amenorrhea in menopause. Three months ago, she began hormone therapy with an estradiol patch, 0.025 mg/24 h, which has alleviated her symptoms completely. However, when taking her cyclic medroxyprogesterone acetate, 5 mg for 12 days of the month, she becomes dizzy, fatigued, and nauseated and has nightmares. She took her last course 1 month ago, but she does not want to take it again. There is no family history or personal history of coronary artery disease, stroke, breast cancer, or colon cancer.

On physical examination, blood pressure is 90/70 mm Hg. Weight is 131 lb (59.5 kg), and height is 63 in (160 cm) (BMI = 23.2 kg/m $ ^{2} $). There are no masses on breast examination. Pelvic examination reveals no ovarian or uterine enlargement.

Ultrasonography demonstrates an endometrial thickness of 6 mm.

Which of the following is the best treatment choice for this patient?
options: Decrease the medroxyprogesterone acetate dosage to 2.5 mg
items.explanation:
## 101 ANSWER: D) Switch to micronized progesterone, 200 mg Progesterone is necessary to protect the

101 Progesterone is necessary to protect the uterus from endometrial hyperplasia and cancer when estrogen is taken for menopausal symptoms in a woman with an intact uterus. Regimens using medroxyprogesterone acetate and micronized progesterone have been demonstrated to prevent endometrial hyperplasia in large studies. Micronized progesterone may be better tolerated and have a less adverse effect on lipids than medroxyprogesterone. Therefore, switching to micronized progesterone, 200 mg for 12 days of the month (Answer D), or 100 mg daily, would be the most appropriate next step.

The medroxyprogesterone acetate regimens that have been demonstrated to prevent endometrial hyperplasia include medroxyprogesterone acetate, 5 or 10 mg for 12 days of the month, or medroxyprogesterone acetate, 2.5 mg daily. There are not enough data to suggest that medroxyprogesterone acetate, 2.5 mg for 12 days of the month (Answer C), adequately prevents endometrial hyperplasia.

If a patient cannot take any type of progesterone by mouth, a levonorgestrel-coated intrauterine device could be considered. In meta-analyses, it has been demonstrated that the levonorgestrel-coated intrauterine device causes the endometrial lining to become atrophic and prevents endometrial hyperplasia. Intravaginal progesterone (Answer E) has also been shown to decrease the incidence of endometrial hyperplasia, but only in small studies. Therefore, it would not be the best treatment choice. In women who use intravaginal progesterone and in women who cannot take progesterone in any form, baseline and yearly endometrial biopsies are required to detect endometrial hyperplasia, with additional endometrial biopsies if there is new-onset vaginal bleeding.

Short-term (<5 years) hormone therapy is safe in a woman with no contraindications, such as personal or family history of breast cancer, thrombotic events, coronary artery disease, or endometrial cancer. This patient has no contraindications to hormone therapy and has had remarkable relief of her symptoms. Therefore, stopping estradiol (Answer A) would not be appropriate. Further, although venlafaxine (Answer B) has been demonstrated to alleviate hot flashes in menopause, it is not as effective as estradiol, and switching to venlafaxine would not be the most appropriate next treatment step.

##### Educational Objective:

Select appropriate hormone therapy to prevent endometrial hyperplasia in a postmenopausal woman.

#### UpToDate Topic Review(s):

Treatment of menopausal symptoms with hormone therapy

##### Reference(s):

Gillet JY, Andre G, Faguer B, et al. Induction of amenorrhea during hormone replacement therapy: optimal micronized progesterone dose. A multicenter study. Maturitas. 1994;19(2):103-115.

Varila E, Wahlström T, Rauramo I. A 5-year follow-up study on the use of a levonorgestrel intrauterine system in women receiving hormone replacement therapy. Fertil Steril. 2001;76(5):969-973.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Question 101: "Select appropriate hormone therapy to prevent endometrial hyperplasia in a postmenopausal woman. #### UpToDate Topic Review(s): Treatment of menopausal symptoms with hormone therapy"

items.question:
A 38-year-old female-to-male transgender individual presents for a new patient appointment. He has been treated with intramuscular testosterone, 200 mg every 2 weeks, for 5 years with satisfactory facial and body hair, deepening of the voice, and enlargement of the clitoris. He also has acne and balding, which are acceptable to him. He has not undergone a mastectomy, hysterectomy, or oophorectomy. There has been no vagin bleeding. He has never smoked cigarettes.

On physical examination, blood pressure is 130/80 mm Hg. Weight is 197 lb (89.5 kg), and height is 66.5 in (168.9 cm) (BMI = 31.3 kg/m²). Skin examination reveals mild acne, male-pattern hair over the face, chest, and extremities and occipital balding. Breasts are without masses. Pelvic examination reveals a small uterus and no adnexal masses.

Laboratory test results:

Total testosterone (mid-dose) = 769 ng/dL (26.7 nmol/L)

Hematocrit = 43% (0.43)

Platelet count = 375 × 10³/μL (375 × 10⁹/L)

Which of the following is the most likely complication of intramuscular testosterone injections if this patient?
items.explanation:
109 ANSWER: E) Erythrocytosis

To achieve masculinization of a female-to-male transgender individual, testosterone is administered to target a normal male range of 300 to 1200 ng/dL (10.4 to 41.6 nmol/L), 1 week after testosterone administration if given intramuscularly, but levels less than 700 ng/dL (24.3 nmol/L) are recommended. Transgender individuals should be seen every 3 months during the first year of treatment, then every 6 to 12 months to monitor testosterone levels; the effectiveness of therapy; and potential adverse effects including erythrocytosis, weight gain, salt retention, lipid abnormalities, cystic acne, and psychological changes. The testosterone level in this individual is greater than 700 ng/dL (24.3 nmol/L) the day before administration of the next testosterone dose, suggesting that his testosterone levels are always higher than the recommended range. Testosterone replacement may cause erythrocytosis (Answer E). The transgender man in this vignette is also overweight and has acne, which might improve slightly with a lower testosterone dosage.

Few transgender men can afford to have a hysterectomy, salpingo-oophorectomy, and mastectomy. Therefore, clinical breast examinations, mammograms, and Papanicolaou testing should be performed at recommended intervals as indicated by the individual's risk group. Testosterone levels in the male range often result in amenorrhea. Female-to-male transgender patients are at risk for endometrial cancer based on aromatization of testosterone to estradiol (Answer D), and endometrial hyperplasia was described in 3 of 19 transgender patients treated with androgens. However, the endometrium is typically atrophic with high-dosage testosterone therapy when examined after hysterectomy and no cases of endometrial cancer have been reported. Therefore, the actual risk of endometrial cancer is unknown.

Liver function abnormalities (Answer A) should not increase with intramuscular testosterone administration, although liver function should be evaluated every 6 months to 1 year because transient elevation in liver enzymes is common.

Low bone mineral density (Answer C) is not a concern if testosterone is maintained in the male range.

Although male-to-female transgender individuals on estrogen therapy are at high risk for thromboembolic events (Answer B), female-to-male transgender individuals are not.

##### Educational Objective:

Monitor hormone levels and potential adverse effects of testosterone therapy in female-to-male transgender patients.

UpToDate Topic Review(s):

Treatment of transsexualism

##### Reference(s):

Committee on Health Care for Underserved Women. Committee Opinion no. 512: health care for transgender individuals. Obstet Gynecol. 2011;118(6):1454-1458.

Hembree WC, Cohen-Kettenis P, Delemarre-van de Waal HA, et al; Endocrine Society. Endocrine treatment of transsexual persons: an Endocrine Society clinical practice guideline. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2009;94(9):3132-3154.

Futterweit W, Deligdisch L. Histopathological effects of exogenously administered testosterone in 19 female-to-male transsexuals. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 1986;62(1):16-21.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Question 109: "Monitor hormone levels and potential adverse effects of testosterone therapy in female-to-male transgender patients."

items.title: Why answer E is correct
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Answer 109: "Monitor hormone levels and potential adverse effects of testosterone therapy in female-to-male transgender patients."

items.title: How to apply this case clinically

items.reference: ESAP 2015 Discussion 109: "Monitor hormone levels and potential adverse effects of testosterone therapy in female-to-male transgender patients."

items.question:
A young woman with classic 21-hydroxylase CAH is transitioning to adult care. On hydrocortisone and fludrocortisone with suppressed renin, she has irregular menses, hirsutism, acne, and elevated testosterone but no adrenal insufficiency symptoms.

Best next step for androgen excess?
items.explanation: Overtreatment with glucocorticoids is not indicated without adrenal insufficiency; combined hormonal contraception suppresses ovarian androgens and protects the endometrium.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 83: "Select appropriate treatment for a woman with classic CAH transitioning to adult care."

items.title: Why answer B is correct
items.text: Overtreatment with glucocorticoids is not indicated without adrenal insufficiency; combined hormonal contraception suppresses ovarian androgens and protects the endometrium.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 83: "Select appropriate treatment for a woman with classic CAH transitioning to adult care."

items.title: How to apply this case clinically
items.text: In adult CAH with adequate glucocorticoid/mineralocorticoid replacement, treat hirsutism with hormonal contraception—not reflex glucocorticoid escalation.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 83: "Select appropriate treatment for a woman with classic CAH transitioning to adult care."

items.statement: Hormonal contraception is first-line for hirsutism in adequately replaced adult CAH.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 83: "Hormonal contraception is the treatment of choice for hirsutism"

items.statement: Rotterdam criteria require two of hyperandrogenism, oligo-anovulation, or polycystic ovaries.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 27: "Rotterdam criteria"

items.statement: Karyotype is indicated in premature ovarian insufficiency.
items.explanation: Turner and FMR1 testing part of workup.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 5: "Karyotype analysis"

items.statement: Micronized progesterone may be better tolerated than medroxyprogesterone.
items.explanation: Fewer mood side effects in some women.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 101: "Micronized progesterone may be better tolerated"

items.statement: Women with CAH need stress steroid plans for labor and delivery.
items.reference: ESAP 2015: "Congenital adrenal hyperplasia"

items.statement: Clomiphene is first-line ovulation induction in PCOS infertility.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 44: "Clomiphene citrate"

items.statement: Spironolactone in women requires reliable contraception.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 83: "must be paired with contraception"

items.explanation: Bicuspid aortic valve and coarctation risk.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 5: "Turner syndrome"

items.assertion: Increasing glucocorticoid to normalize 17-OHP is always correct in adult CAH with hirsutism.
items.reason: Overtreatment causes Cushing; OCPs treat androgen excess.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 83: "increasing the glucocorticoid dosage is not the best choice"

items.assertion: Elevated testosterone alone diagnoses PCOS without other criteria.
items.reason: Need two Rotterdam criteria after excluding other causes.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 27: "two out of three"

items.assertion: Normal FSH in amenorrhea excludes ovarian failure.
items.reason: Single FSH may fluctuate; repeat and karyotype if concerned.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 5: "primary ovarian insufficiency"

items.assertion: Estrogen alone is sufficient in women with intact uterus.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 101: "Progesterone is necessary to protect the uterus"

items.assertion: Fludrocortisone dose should increase when renin is suppressed and BP normal.
items.reason: Suppressed renin suggests adequate mineralocorticoid replacement.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 83: "renin activity is suppressed"

items.assertion: Metformin is first-line for all PCOS hirsutism.
items.reason: OCPs and antiandrogens treat hirsutism; metformin role is metabolic.
items.reference: ESAP 2015: "Polycystic ovary syndrome"

items.assertion: Letrozole is never used for PCOS ovulation induction.
items.reason: Letrozole is used off-label/approved in some settings for ovulation.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 44: "fertility treatment"

items.assertion: Complete androgen insensitivity presents with female phenotype and undescended testes.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 5: "androgen insensitivity"
